items.title: Classification by HHO axis
items.contentHtml:
Classification according to hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis

  • Functional hypothalamic amenorrhea: due to gonadotropin-releasing hormone deficiency due to stress, weight loss or excessive exercise; presents with low or normal luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone, low estradiol.
  • Pituitary amenorrhea: due to injury (prolactinoma, postpartum necrosis) with gonadotropin deficiency; prolactin may be elevated.
  • Ovarian amenorrhea: primary ovarian failure (elevated follicle-stimulating hormone >40 IU/L, low estradiol) or polycystic ovary syndrome (luteinizing hormone/follicle-stimulating hormone ratio >2).
  • Uterine amenorrhea: alteration of the endometrium or Müllerian ducts. (Asherman syndrome, Müller agenesis) with normal hormonal axis.

items.foco: Most frequent causes of primary amenorrhea
items.title: Common causes of primary amenorrhea
items.contentHtml:
Most frequent causes of primary amenorrhea

  • Gonadal dysgenesis (Turner syndrome, 45, breasts and pubic hair, karyotype 46,XX.
  • Imperforate hymen or transverse vaginal septum: primary amenorrhea with cyclical pain and hematocolpos.
  • Constitutional developmental delay: transient functional hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.
  • Complete androgen insensitivity: karyotype 46,XY, intra-abdominal testicles, absence of pubic hair, developed breasts.

items.title: Diagnostic criteria for primary amenorrhea
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic criterion for primary amenorrhea

  • Absence of menarche at 15 years with development of normal secondary sexual characteristics.
  • Absence of menarche at 13 years if there is no development of breasts or pubic hair.
  • Primary amenorrhea is also considered if at 3-5 years after the thelarche there is no menarche has occurred.
  • In the initial evaluation, the following should be requested: follicle-stimulating hormone, luteinizing hormone, estradiol, prolactin, testosterone, and karyotype if there is short stature or stigmata of Turner syndrome.
  • Pelvic ultrasound rules out obstructive Müllerian anomalies or uterine agenesis.

items.foco: Asherman syndrome as a secondary cause
items.title: Asherman syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Asherman syndrome as a secondary cause

  • It is secondary amenorrhea due to intrauterine synechiae that obliterate the endometrial cavity, usually after uterine curettage (postpartum, postabortion) or pelvic infection.
  • The hormonal axis is normal: follicle-stimulating hormone, luteinizing hormone, estradiol and prolactin within ranges.
  • The estrogen-progestogen test does not cause bleeding due to the absence of functioning endometrium.
  • The definitive diagnosis is made by hysteroscopy, which also allows the lysis of adhesions.
  • Hysterosalpingography shows irregular filling defects in the uterine cavity.

items.title: Progesterone test
items.contentHtml:
Progesterone test in secondary amenorrhea

  • Administer medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg/day for 5-7 days or micronized progesterone 200-300 mg/day orally.
  • If withdrawal bleeding appears in the following 2-7 days, it indicates that the endometrium was exposed to estrogens (functioning ovarian axis) and the amenorrhea is due to anovulation (e.g. polycystic ovary syndrome).
  • Negative test (no bleeding): suggests severe estrogen deficiency (ovarian or hypothalamic failure) or endometrial alteration (Asherman syndrome).
  • In practice, the progesterone test indicates type 2 amenorrhea (anovulatory with estrogen present) vs type 1 (hypoestrogenic).

items.foco: Elevation of FSH indicates ovarian failure
items.title: elevated FSH = ovarian failure
items.contentHtml:
Elevated follicle stimulating hormone indicates ovarian failure

  • Elevated follicle stimulating hormone (FSH) (>40 IU/L on two occasions at least 1 month apart) in a woman <40 years old defines primary ovarian insufficiency (premature ovarian failure).
  • In secondary amenorrhea, FSH >40 IU/L with estradiol <20 pg/mL confirms irreversible ovarian failure (menopause).
  • In primary amenorrhea, elevated FSH suggests gonadal dysgenesis (Turner syndrome).
  • Low anti-Müllerian hormone (<1 ng/mL) and low inhibin B support the diagnosis of depletion follicular.

items.foco: Hyperprolactinemia and its relationship with amenorrhea
items.title: Hyperprolactinemia and amenorrhea
items.contentHtml:
Hyperprolactinemia and its relationship with amenorrhea

  • Elevated prolactin (>25 ng/mL in non-pregnant women) inhibits the pulsatile secretion of gonadotropin-releasing hormone, causing functional hypothalamic amenorrhea with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.
  • Hyperprolactinemia is cause of secondary amenorrhea in approximately 15-20% of cases.
  • It is frequently associated with galactorrhea (milk secretion not related to lactation).
  • The most common causes: prolactinoma (pituitary micro or macroadenoma), drugs (antipsychotics, metoclopramide), primary hypothyroidism (due to elevated hormone-releasing hormone). thyrotropin).

items.title: Polycystic ovary syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Polycystic ovary syndrome and amenorrhea

  • Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is the most common cause of secondary amenorrhea due to chronic anovulation.
  • Diagnostic criteria (Rotterdam): at least 2 of 3: oligo/anovulation, clinical hyperandrogenism or biochemical , and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound (>12 follicles of 2-9 mm or ovarian volume >10 mL).
  • The progesterone test is usually positive (withdrawal bleeding) due to adequate estrogen exposure.
  • The luteinizing hormone/follicle stimulating hormone ratio is >2-3 characteristic but not necessary for diagnosis.

items.foco: Müllerian duct anomalies in primary school
items.title: Müllerian anomalies in primary amenorrhea
items.contentHtml:
Müllerian duct anomalies in primary amenorrhea

  • The Müllerian anomalies are the cause of primary amenorrhea with normal breast development and normal pubic hair, 46,XX karyotype, and normal hormone levels.
  • The agenesis of the Müllerian ducts (Müllerian duct syndrome) Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser) presents an absent or rudimentary uterus, a shortened or absent vagina, and functional ovaries.
  • Others: imperforate hymen, transverse vaginal septum (with hematocolpos, cyclical pain).
  • The diagnosis is confirmed with pelvic ultrasound or magnetic resonance imaging; Renal evaluation is mandatory due to association with unilateral renal agenesis.

items.title: Galactorrhea-amenorrhea due to prolactinoma
items.contentHtml:
Galactorrhea-amenorrhea due to prolactinoma

  • Prolactinoma is the most common prolactin-secreting pituitary tumor; causes galactorrhea-amenorrhea syndrome.
  • Amenorrhea is secondary, with serum prolactin >200 ng/mL suggesting macroadenoma (>1 cm); Levels between 50-200 ng/mL may correspond to microadenoma.
  • First-line treatment is dopamine agonists (cabergoline 0.25-0.5 mg twice a week; bromocriptine 2.5-5 mg/day).
  • Normalization of prolactin restores ovulation and menstruation in most patients. cases.

items.title: Hirsutism and amenorrhea = hyperandrogenism
items.contentHtml:
Hirsutism and amenorrhea suggest hyperandrogenism

  • The presence of hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey score ≥8) together with amenorrhea points to causes of hyperandrogenism.
  • polycystic ovary syndrome is the most common etiology. common; also non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia (deficiency of 21-hydroxylase, 17-hydroxyprogesterone >2 ng/mL basal or after ACTH), androgen-secreting ovarian or adrenal tumor (testosterone >200 ng/dL).
  • insulin resistance is associated with hyperandrogenism and anovulation; request fasting blood glucose and insulin.

items.title: Estrogen-progestin test
items.contentHtml:
Estrogen-progestogen test for endometrial integrity

  • estradiol 2 mg/day or ethinyl estradiol 0.02-0.05 mg/day is administered for 21-25 days, adding medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg/day the last 7-10 days.
  • If withdrawal bleeding appears, it confirms endometrial integrity and response to estrogen and progesterone (functional endometrium).
  • If there is no bleeding, it suggests endometrial alteration (Asherman syndrome, Müller agenesis) or lack of previous estrogen exposure (in primary amenorrhea with a uterus present).
  • This test allows differentiation. uterine amenorrhea from hypoestrogenic amenorrhea when the progesterone test was negative.
